title: Multiple Fatty Lumps on Dogs: Understanding Lipomas and Triage
meta_desc: Learn about multiple fatty lumps in dogs (lipomas), when they are considered an emergency, and why visual documentation helps your veterinarian.
content_html:
What are these fatty lumps?

In many cases, soft and movable lumps found under a dog's skin are identified as lipomas. Lipomas are benign, non-cancerous tumors composed of mature fat cells. While they can appear as a single mass, many dogs develop multiple lipomas across their chest, abdomen, and limbs as they reach middle to senior age.

Is this an emergency?

The urgency level for multiple fatty lumps is generally Low. Because lipomas are typically slow-growing and non-invasive, they do not require immediate emergency intervention. However, it is important to have any new growth evaluated by a veterinarian during a routine appointment to rule out more serious conditions, such as mast cell tumors or sarcomas, which can occasionally mimic the feel of a fatty mass.

Why a photo helps with triage

Providing a high-quality photo or video of the lumps to your veterinary team can be a vital part of the triage process. A visual record allows the vet to assess the location, approximate size, and skin condition surrounding the mass. Comparing photos over time also helps track growth rates, which is crucial information for determining whether a biopsy or surgical removal is necessary.

faqs.question: Can I tell if a lump is a lipoma just by feeling it?
faqs.answer: While lipomas have a characteristic soft, 'squishy' feel, it is impossible to be 100% certain through touch alone. A veterinarian must perform a Fine Needle Aspirate (FNA) to confirm the lump is made of fat cells.

faqs.question: When should I be worried about a fatty lump?
faqs.answer: You should contact your vet if a lump grows rapidly, becomes firm or fixed to the underlying tissue, appears red or irritated, or if it seems to cause your dog pain.

faqs.question: Will these lumps eventually disappear on their own?
faqs.answer: Lipomas generally do not go away on their own. While weight loss can sometimes reduce their size, they typically persist and may slowly enlarge over time.
